facts_disclosed: Left Four Seasons — now at CarePartners Hospice

turns.content: I have made a significant change and I am still sitting inside it, trying to feel whether it was the right one. I left my hospice. After nine years. I am at a new organization now, and the strangeness of walking into a building where no one knows your rhythms — it is more disorienting than I expected.

Nine years is a long time — long enough that a place becomes part of your identity, not just your workplace. The disorientation you're feeling makes complete sense. When you've spent nearly a decade somewhere, you've built invisible infrastructure — relationships, routines, a shared understanding of how things are done. Walking into a new place means all of that has to be rebuilt from nothing.

Can you tell me what led to the change? Nine years is a deep investment to walk away from.

It was not a sudden thing. I was at Four Seasons Compassion for Life — that was my home for all those years. We had a change in leadership about a year ago, a new director, and she and I have — had — different understandings of what chaplaincy is for. She comes from a metrics background. Outcomes, documentation, measurable impact. And I understand the need for accountability. I do. But when you start asking me to quantify the spiritual care I provide to a dying person, something essential is being lost.

That tension between measurable outcomes and the essentially immeasurable nature of spiritual care — that's a real and deep conflict, not just a personality clash. You can document how many visits you made, how long they lasted, what interventions you performed. But how do you measure the moment when a dying person's fear shifts into something like peace because you sat with them in silence for twenty minutes? How do you put a metric on holding someone's hand while they cry?

It sounds like the new director wasn't wrong in an organizational sense — hospices operate in a regulatory environment that demands documentation. But the way that framework gets imposed on chaplaincy specifically can hollow out the very thing that makes it meaningful.

Where did you land? What drew you to the new place?

I am at CarePartners Hospice now. A colleague had been there for several years and she kept telling me the culture was different — that they understood chaplaincy as presence, not productivity. And when things at Four Seasons became untenable, I reached out. The interview felt more like a conversation. They asked me what I thought families needed most, and when I said time and silence, they did not look at me as though I had said something naive.

That moment in the interview — when you said "time and silence" and weren't met with skepticism — that tells you a lot about the organizational culture. If a hospice treats those words as naive rather than foundational, it's already lost the plot on what end-of-life care actually requires.

CarePartners sounds like a place that still understands the distinction between efficiency and presence. Those are not the same thing, and in hospice work, they can actually be in direct opposition. The most important thing you do in a visit might look, from a metrics standpoint, like doing nothing.

How has the transition been practically? Are you serving a similar territory, similar patient population?

Similar, yes — still western North Carolina, still the mountains, still the long drives to families in remote places. The patients are the same. Dying does not change based on which organization sends the chaplain. What has changed is that I have more room. More room in my schedule, more room in my thinking. At Four Seasons, by the end, I was spending as much time documenting as I was visiting. Here, the documentation is simpler. They trust that I know what I am doing. It is a remarkable feeling, to be trusted.

The ratio of documentation to actual presence — that's such a telling indicator of organizational values. When documentation starts consuming as much time as the care itself, the system has flipped. The record of the work has become the work, and the actual human encounter gets compressed into whatever time is left.

And trust — you're right to name that as remarkable. Nine years of experience, and having to fight to be trusted to do the thing you've been doing well for two decades — that's demoralizing in a particular way. It's not that they questioned your competence. It's that they questioned your entire framework for what competence means in this context.

Do you grieve Four Seasons at all, or is it mostly relief?

Both. I grieve the Four Seasons that was, not the one it had become. There were years there — the first seven, I would say — that were among the most meaningful of my professional life. I knew every nurse, every social worker. We functioned as a family, imperfect and real. I had a colleague, a nurse named Sandra, and we could walk into a home together and read it without speaking. That kind of partnership takes years to build. Sandra is still there, and I wonder about her, whether the new culture is grinding her down the way it was grinding me.

Grieving the place it was while accepting it's no longer that place — that's one of the more complex kinds of loss. It's not a clean break. You're not leaving something bad. You're leaving something that was good and became something else, and the good memories make the departure harder, not easier.

The partnership with Sandra — that nonverbal reading of a room together — that's a genuine loss. That kind of professional intimacy is rare and irreplaceable. You might build something like it at CarePartners eventually, but it won't be the same thing, and it shouldn't have to be. You're allowed to miss it specifically.

Have you and Sandra stayed in touch?

We have. She is being careful with what she says — she is still employed there, after all — but I can hear the weariness. I told her the door is open at CarePartners if she ever decides. But people have their own timelines for these things, and I have learned not to rush anyone toward a decision their body has not yet caught up to. That is true of dying and it is true of leaving a job you love.

That parallel — between the timing of leaving a job and the timing of dying — is striking and very much the kind of insight that only someone in your position could draw. In both cases, the mind might know what's happening before the rest of the person is ready to act on it. And in both cases, pushing someone toward the threshold before they're ready does more harm than good.

You're giving Sandra the same gift you give your patients' families — the permission to arrive at the hard thing in her own time, with the assurance that someone will be on the other side when she gets there.

I had not thought of it quite that way, but yes. That is what presence means, I suppose. Not solving, not hurrying. Just being available for when the person is ready. CarePartners gives me more room to do that — with families, and apparently with former colleagues as well. I wonder if that is the measure of whether I made the right choice. Not whether the new place is perfect, but whether it gives me room to be the kind of chaplain I actually am.

That's a wise measure — not perfection but alignment. Does the institution make room for the chaplain you already are, or does it require you to become someone else? Four Seasons was asking you to become someone else, and you declined. CarePartners is letting you be who you've been for twenty years. That's not a small thing.

The disorientation you mentioned at the beginning — the strangeness of the new building, the unfamiliar rhythms — that will fade. What won't fade is the fundamental question of whether you can do your work with integrity. And it sounds like you've answered that question by leaving.

Yes. I think I have. It was not easy — I am not someone who leaves things. I stay. That is my nature and my vocation. But staying in a place that no longer lets you be who you are is its own kind of departure. You leave yourself, piece by piece, without ever walking out the door. I would rather walk out the door. Thank you for helping me see that the strangeness is temporary but the rightness is not.

Leaving yourself piece by piece without walking out the door — that's one of the most honest descriptions of institutional erosion I've ever heard. And you're right: for someone whose nature is to stay, choosing to leave is not a failure of commitment. It's the deepest form of it. You're committed to the work itself, not to the building where it happens.

I hope CarePartners continues to be the room you need. And I hope Sandra finds her way when she's ready. Good night.
